content.text: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked APIs. A REST API exposes resources — entities like users, tasks, agents, or runs — as URL endpoints, and uses standard HTTP methods to perform operations on them. When both client and server agree on this uniform interface, any programming language or tool that speaks HTTP can consume the API without additional integration logic.

REST was defined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It builds on six constraints: client-server separation, statelessness, cacheability, a uniform interface, a layered system, and optional code-on-demand. In practice, modern 'REST' APIs focus primarily on the first four constraints. Each request must carry all the information the server needs; no session state lives on the server side between requests.

REST APIs return data (usually JSON) rather than rendered HTML. A single server can power a web browser, a mobile app, and an automated agent simultaneously — all consuming the same endpoints. This separation of data from presentation is what makes REST the backbone of modern distributed systems.

Before REST, applications often used SOAP, XML-RPC, or proprietary protocols. Interoperability required complex contract negotiation and specialised tooling. REST replaced that with a convention built on HTTP — a protocol already understood by every networking stack on Earth. This dramatically lowered the cost of integration.

For AI agents and agentic pipelines, REST APIs are the primary mechanism for connecting components. An orchestrator calls a REST endpoint to launch a task, poll its status, retrieve results, or trigger a tool. Microservice architectures are built from REST (or gRPC) services. Every cloud provider's management plane — spinning up infrastructure, storing models, reading logs — exposes a REST API.

Understanding how to build and consume REST APIs correctly is therefore a foundational skill for anyone building production-grade AI systems. Mistakes in status codes, error payloads, or authentication design compound quickly as the number of consumers grows.
any client that speaks HTTP can consume the API — Python, JavaScript, curl, Go, mobile apps.
because no session state exists on the server, any server instance can handle any request. Horizontal scaling is straightforward.
GET responses can be cached by CDNs, browsers, or reverse proxies, reducing load without code changes.
REST conventions (nouns as paths, verbs as HTTP methods) are widely understood. FastAPI auto-generates OpenAPI docs.
Postman, httpx, curl, OpenAPI generators, and dozens of API gateways understand REST natively.
you can expose one REST endpoint at a time and evolve the API without breaking existing consumers if you version carefully.
a fixed endpoint either returns too many fields (waste) or too few (requiring a second call). GraphQL addresses this, at the cost of complexity.
REST is request-response. Push notifications require Server-Sent Events, WebSockets, or polling — each is an additional pattern on top of REST.
fetching related resources (an agent and its latest run) can require sequential requests unless you design compound endpoints or use query parameters to embed related data.
actions that do not map cleanly to CRUD ("retry this run", "cancel that job") are awkward. Common workarounds are POST to a sub-resource (
breaking changes require a versioning strategy (URL prefix v1/v2, headers, query params) implemented by the team.

Every REST interaction is a stateless HTTP request. The client sends a request line (method + URL), headers, and an optional body. The server processes the request and returns a status code, headers, and a response body.

HTTP methods map to the four CRUD operations: GET retrieves a resource without side effects; POST creates a new resource and returns 201 Created with a Location header pointing to the new resource; PUT replaces a resource entirely; PATCH updates it partially; DELETE removes it and returns 204 No Content.
URLs represent resources as nouns, never verbs.
is the nested collection of runs belonging to that agent. Hierarchical nesting beyond two levels often signals that a query parameter is more appropriate.
The response body is almost always JSON for modern APIs. The Content-Type header should be
. Error responses must also be JSON with a consistent shape: at minimum a

HTTP status codes communicate outcome semantics: 2xx means success, 4xx means the client made an error (bad input, missing auth, not found), 5xx means the server failed. Using the correct code lets clients handle errors generically without parsing the body.

The diagram below shows the full lifecycle of a REST request: the client selects a method and path, middleware layers process auth/rate-limiting/logging, the router dispatches to the correct handler, the handler queries the data layer, and a JSON response returns to the client.

attrs.alt: REST API request-response lifecycle: client HTTP methods → middleware → route handlers → data layer → JSON response

content.text: REST is the right choice when you need a synchronous request-response interface, your consumers are diverse (different languages, teams, or public developers), and the data model is resource-oriented. CRUD APIs for managing entities — agents, users, jobs, files — are the canonical use case.

Choose REST over gRPC when you need browser compatibility, public documentation (OpenAPI), or human-readable traffic inspection. Choose WebSockets instead of REST when the primary use case is bidirectional streaming or low-latency push. Choose GraphQL when many client types need different field subsets from complex nested data.

Avoid REST for high-throughput binary streaming (video, sensor data), tight latency budgets where HTTP overhead matters, or systems where the contract must be machine-enforced at compile time (gRPC with generated stubs gives stronger guarantees).
Step 1 — Define your resources and URL scheme
List the entities your API manages. Write their base URLs and the HTTP method for each operation before writing any code. For an agent-orchestration service:
Step 2 — Define request and response schemas

Use Pydantic models (FastAPI) or marshmallow/dataclasses to declare exactly what fields are required, optional, and their types. Schema validation eliminates an entire class of bugs and automatically generates OpenAPI documentation.

Each handler function receives the validated request data, performs business logic (usually a database query or service call), and returns the response. Keep handlers thin — put logic in service functions that are independently testable.
Use API keys for machine-to-machine, OAuth2 + JWT for user-facing. FastAPI's dependency injection makes it straightforward to declare auth as a reusable dependency injected into any endpoint.
Step 5 — Test with httpx and pytest
for async tests. Test the happy path, validation errors, auth failures, and not-found cases for every endpoint.

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query, Depends, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy import select, func
from app.db import get_session
from app.models import Run, Agent

app = FastAPI()

class RunOut(BaseModel):
    id: str
    agent_id: str
    status: str
    tokens_used: int

    class Config:
        from_attributes = True

class PagedRuns(BaseModel):
    items: list[RunOut]
    total: int
    page: int
    page_size: int

@app.get("/agents/{agent_id}/runs", response_model=PagedRuns)
async def list_runs(
    agent_id: str,
    status: Optional[str] = Query(None, description="Filter by status"),
    page: int = Query(1, ge=1),
    page_size: int = Query(20, ge=1, le=100),
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_session),
):
    # Verify agent exists
    agent = await db.get(Agent, agent_id)
    if agent is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Agent not found")

    stmt = select(Run).where(Run.agent_id == agent_id)
    if status:
        stmt = stmt.where(Run.status == status)

    total_stmt = select(func.count()).select_from(stmt.subquery())
    total = await db.scalar(total_stmt)

    stmt = stmt.offset((page - 1) * page_size).limit(page_size)
    result = await db.execute(stmt)
    runs = result.scalars().all()

    return PagedRuns(
        items=runs,
        total=total,
        page=page,
        page_size=page_size,
    )

A production AI orchestration service exposes a REST API that external systems use to submit tasks to a multi-agent pipeline. The API accepts a task description, validates it against a Pydantic schema, persists it to PostgreSQL, publishes a message to a Redis task queue, and returns a 202 Accepted response with a task ID. Clients poll
to check status, or subscribe to

from fastapi import FastAPI, BackgroundTasks, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from enum import Enum
import asyncio, redis.asyncio as aioredis

app = FastAPI()
redis = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost")

class TaskStatus(str, Enum):
    queued = "queued"
    running = "running"
    complete = "complete"
    failed = "failed"

class TaskCreate(BaseModel):
    prompt: str
    model: str = "gpt-4o"
    max_steps: int = 10

class TaskResponse(BaseModel):
    id: str
    status: TaskStatus

@app.post("/tasks", response_model=TaskResponse, status_code=202)
async def submit_task(body: TaskCreate, bg: BackgroundTasks):
    task_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    await redis.set(f"task:{task_id}:status", TaskStatus.queued.value)
    # Enqueue to worker pool via Redis list
    payload = json.dumps({"id": task_id, **body.model_dump()})
    await redis.lpush("task_queue", payload)
    return TaskResponse(id=task_id, status=TaskStatus.queued)

@app.get("/tasks/{task_id}", response_model=TaskResponse)
async def get_task(task_id: str):
    status = await redis.get(f"task:{task_id}:status")
    if status is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Task not found")
    return TaskResponse(id=task_id, status=TaskStatus(status.decode()))
. These mix HTTP methods with URL-level verbs, doubling the surface area. Use noun-based paths and let HTTP verbs carry the action:
. When a breaking change is needed, add
. Default page_size should be small (20–100); max should be capped (1000).
follows the HTTP spec and makes client caching straightforward.
Use Pydantic for all input and output:
schema validation is never optional in production — it prevents data corruption and auto-generates docs.
in endpoint decorators so clients know what to expect.
for POST endpoints that create resources, accept an optional
header. Store and replay the response for duplicate requests to prevent double-creates.

REST APIs scale horizontally because they are stateless. Add more server instances behind a load balancer; any instance can handle any request. The bottleneck is almost always the database, not the web tier.
Cache GET responses aggressively. FastAPI running behind nginx or a CDN can serve thousands of identical requests from cache. Add
headers to resources that change infrequently (agent metadata, model lists). Use ETags or
For write-heavy endpoints, keep handlers async and avoid blocking I/O. Use
or SQLAlchemy's async session rather than synchronous drivers. Use connection pooling (pgbouncer for PostgreSQL, redis connection pools) to avoid connection exhaustion under load.

before deploying. A naive FastAPI app with async PostgreSQL can handle 5,000–15,000 requests per second on a single instance; the ceiling drops sharply with synchronous database drivers or CPU-bound handlers.
the de-facto standard for Python REST APIs. Pydantic-first, async-native, OpenAPI auto-generation.
mature, synchronous option with good extension support. Better for smaller projects or teams already on Flask.
batteries-included REST layer for Django. Best when the project uses Django ORM.
async HTTP client for consuming REST APIs and for testing FastAPI with
schema validation, serialisation, and OpenAPI schema generation.
ORM with full async support via
integration test framework for async REST endpoints.
Complex nested data, many client types

Never expose a REST API without authentication. For machine-to-machine use API keys: a random 256-bit token stored hashed (bcrypt or SHA-256) in the database, checked on every request. For user-facing APIs use OAuth 2.0 + JWT: short-lived access tokens (15 min) and long-lived refresh tokens stored in HTTP-only cookies.

from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException, Header
from app.db import get_db, APIKey
import secrets, hashlib

async def require_api_key(
    x_api_key: str = Header(...),
    db=Depends(get_db),
):
    key_hash = hashlib.sha256(x_api_key.encode()).hexdigest()
    key_obj = await db.get_api_key_by_hash(key_hash)
    if key_obj is None or not key_obj.active:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid API key")
    return key_obj
Protect every public endpoint with rate limits. Use
(a fastapi-compatible rate limiter) or an API gateway (Kong, nginx) for this. Return 429 Too Many Requests with a
header when the limit is reached.
Pydantic schema validation eliminates most injection vectors at the boundary. Never pass raw user strings into SQL queries — use ORM parameterised queries or
with bound parameters. Validate that IDs are the expected format (UUID, integer) before querying.
All traffic must go over HTTPS in production. Configure CORS explicitly — do not use
in production. List allowed origins, methods, and headers explicitly.
Think of a REST API as a filing cabinet with a very well-organised labelling system. Each drawer is a resource collection (
). Each file inside is an individual resource (
). The HTTP methods are the actions you perform on those files: GET reads the file, POST drops in a new one, PUT replaces an existing file with a new version, DELETE shreds it.

The HTTP status code is the filing clerk's response: 200 means 'found it and here it is', 201 means 'filed the new document', 404 means 'no such file', 422 means 'I can't accept this document — it's the wrong form', 500 means 'the filing room is on fire'. Every REST client in the world understands this language without any custom documentation.
REST maps HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) to CRUD operations on noun-based URLs — verbs belong in HTTP methods, not paths.
Status codes carry semantics: 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. Never return 200 with an error body.
Always validate input with Pydantic; it prevents data corruption and auto-generates OpenAPI documentation.
Paginate all list endpoints; returning unbounded lists breaks under load and exposes the full dataset.
REST is stateless — scale horizontally by adding server instances behind a load balancer; the database is the bottleneck.
Secure every endpoint with authentication (API key or JWT) and rate limiting; CORS must be explicitly configured.
FastAPI + Pydantic + SQLAlchemy async is the recommended Python stack for new REST services: type-safe, fast, and self-documenting.
